data.narrative:
Star Catcher, a Series A space and aerospace company founded by Andrew Rush, Michael Snyder, and Bryan Lyandvert, has raised $88M to date. The valuation universe comprises 5 public comparables (RKLB, PL, LUNR, ASTS, SATL) trading at a median 25.8x revenue and 8 private peers including SpaceX, Sierra Space, and Planet Labs, though the narrow same-stage peer set lacks sufficient depth for direct median benchmarking.

Stage-progression methodology is the primary applicable framework. Using total raised ($88M) as a proxy for post-money valuation at Series A entry, a 2.0x step-up multiplier—consistent with typical Series A-to-Series B progression in the sector—yields $176M. Public-comp anchoring cannot be applied without disclosed revenue figures; private companies rarely disclose this metric. Peer-median comparison is inapplicable due to insufficient same-stage and adjacent-stage comps meeting minimum density thresholds (0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, below the 3-datapoint floor).

The analysis supports a valuation range of $150M to $202M, with a midpoint of $176M. This reflects a conservative stage-progression approach and carries material uncertainty. Key limitations include reliance on a single methodology, absence of revenue-based cross-checks typical of mature-stage analysis, and a thin private-peer set for calibration. Additional revenue or growth-rate disclosure would materially strengthen confidence in any valuation estimate within this range.
